coredata.dc:title: Smartphone-assisted visual ratio-fluorescence detection of hypochlorite based on copper nanoclusters
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yanyue
He, Yu
Ge, Yili
Song, Gongwu
Zhou, Jiangang
coredata.dc:description: A sensitive naked eye and ratio-fluorescence sensor for Curcumin (CCM) and hypochlorite (ClO-) determination based on copper nanoclusters (Cu NCs) was developed. The fluorescence of the Cu NCs can be quenched due to inner filter effect (IFE) between CCM and Cu NCs, and the ratio fluorescence probe was formed. After adding ClO- to Cu NCs-CCM system, the phenolic and methoxy groups of CCM were oxidized to quinones, then the fluorescence of CCM was quenched and the fluorescence of Cu NC was restored. Moreover, the continuous detection of CCM and ClO- is accompanied by the change of solution color. Therefore, CCM and ClO- semiquantitative visual and fluorescence dual channel detection were realized. The detection results show that the detection based on Cu NCs-CCM probe has a wide detection range (0–412 µM) and low detection limit (24 µM), and a good recovery rate is obtained in adulterated milk and tap water detection. Furthermore, smartphone was introduced for image digital colorimetric analysis through the acquisition, recognition and RGB data processing of solution colors, providing an effective scheme for the field rapid detection of hypochlorite.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Copper nanoclusters
Naked eye detection
Ratio-fluorescence
Curcumin
Hypochlorite
Smartphone

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Ratio- fluorescence detection of CCM with Cu NCs
Fluorescence assay of ClO- with Cu NCs-CCM system
Selectivity analysis for CCM and ClO-
Principle for CCM and ClO- analysis using Cu NCs
Detection of ClO- in tap water and milk

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Fluorescence spectrum of Cu NCs. Insert: photographs of Cu NCs in water under natural light and 365 nm UV light (right); (b) TEM image of Cu NCs; inset: HRTEM images of Cu NCs with the crystalline structure; (c) FTIR spectra of Cu NCs and ovalbumin; (d) Cu 2p spectrum in Cu NCs.

(a) Fluorescence intensity of mixed solution including: Cu NCs; Cu NCs, and NaClO; Cu NCs, CCM; Cu NCs, CCM, and NaClO. The concentration of CCM is 5 µM, NaClO is 412 µM; (b) UV–vis spectra of CCM, excitation and emission spectrum of Cu NCs.
Fluorescence spectra of CCM in the presence of ClO- with different concentrations.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of CuNCs with different CCM concentrations from 0 to 5 μM under 340 nm excitation wavelength; (b) Linear relationship of the ratiometric fluorescence intensity F536/F436 versus different concentrations from 0 to 5 μM; (c) The digital photo of the sensing system Cu NCs in the presence of various levels of CCM (0, 0.5, 1.5, 2.0, 3.0, 3.5, 4.0, 4.5, 5.0 µM) taken with a smartphone under daylight and 365 nm uv-light.

(a) Fluorescence spectrum of CuNCs-CCM in anhydrous ethanol with different ClO- concentrations (from 0 to 412 µM); (b) scatter diagram and the fitting curve between the ratiometric fluorescence intensity F436/F536) and different ClO- concentrations in the range from 0 to 0.73 µM (c) and 0.73 to 412 µM (d); (e) The digital photo of the sensing system CuNCs-CCM in the presence of various levels of ClO- (0, 20, 50, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300, 350, 400 µM) taken with a smartphone under daylight and 365 nm UV-light.
The selectivity of fluorescent probe CuNCs-CCM toward various potential interfering substances.

(a) scatter diagram and the fitting curve between the R/B value of CuNCs and different CCM concentrations in the range from 0.5 to 5 µM; (c) scatter diagram and the fitting curve between the R/B value of CuNCs-CCM and different ClO- concentrations in the range from 0 to 150 µM.
Schematic illustration of smartphone-assisted ratio-fluorescence detection of ClO- based on CuNCs.
Comparison of different methods for ClO- determination.

head.ce:title: Smartphone-assisted visual ratio-fluorescence detection of hypochlorite based on copper nanoclusters

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hubei Collaborative Innovation Center for Advanced Organic Chemical Materials, Ministry-of-Education Key Laboratory for the Synthesis and Application of Organic Functional Molecules, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hubei University, Wuhan 430062, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Collaborative Innovation Center for Advanced Organic Chemical Materials, Ministry-of-Education Key Laboratory for the Synthesis and Application of Organic Functional Molecules
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hubei Collaborative Innovation Center for Advanced Organic Chemical Materials, Ministry-of-Education Key Laboratory for the Synthesis and Application of Organic Functional Molecules, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hubei University, Wuhan 430062, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A sensitive naked eye and ratio-fluorescence sensor for Curcumin (CCM) and hypochlorite (ClO-) determination based on copper nanoclusters (Cu NCs) was developed. The fluorescence of the Cu NCs can be quenched due to inner filter effect (IFE) between CCM and Cu NCs, and the ratio fluorescence probe was formed. After adding ClO- to Cu NCs-CCM system, the phenolic and methoxy groups of CCM were oxidized to quinones, then the fluorescence of CCM was quenched and the fluorescence of Cu NC was restored. Moreover, the continuous detection of CCM and ClO- is accompanied by the change of solution color. Therefore, CCM and ClO- semiquantitative visual and fluorescence dual channel detection were realized. The detection results show that the detection based on Cu NCs-CCM probe has a wide detection range (0–412 µM) and low detection limit (24 µM), and a good recovery rate is obtained in adulterated milk and tap water detection. Furthermore, smartphone was introduced for image digital colorimetric analysis through the acquisition, recognition and RGB data processing of solution colors, providing an effective scheme for the field rapid detection of hypochlorite.

ce:section.ce:para: Metal nanoclusters have been receiving wide attention in recent years due to their excellent optical stability, low toxicity and good biocompatibility [1–3]. Different nanoclusters such as gold, silver and copper nanoclusters have been applied in biosensors, cell markers, bioimaging and other fields [4–6]. Owing to rich content and low cost, much more effort have been devoted exploring copper nanoclusters (Cu NCs) for detecting analytes including ions, molecules and enzyme [7–10]. The main sensing mechanisms can generally be divided into three types: fluorescence quenching, fluorescence enhancement and ratio fluorescence. Most of the researches on fluorescent probes are based on the change of single-wavelength emission intensity, such as fluorescence quenching or enhancing probes [11–13]. These single-wavelength emitting fluorescent probes are susceptible to factors such as photo bleaching, the concentration of probe, microenvironment, and stability under light exposure. However, ratio-fluorescence methods can overcome above drawbacks. The ratio-fluorescence method employed the intensity ratio of two emission peaks to detect the target, which can effectively increase the response range and avoid the influence of environment [14–16]. Therefore, it is of great significance to design a ratio-fluorescence method to improve the sensing performance of Cu NCs.

Hypochlorous acid is widely used to disinfect surfaces, fabrics and other contaminated objects, as well as water, fruits, vegetables and utensils [17–19]. The hypochlorite ion (ClO-) can cause human diseases due to excessive presence in the human body or excessive inhalation in life [20,21]. In addition, some manufacturers add ClO- to milk as a preservative to prevent microbial infection and reproduction, delaying food spoilage and extending product shelf life [22–24]. However, excessive amounts of ClO- can destroy organic compounds in milk that have side effects on the body.[25] Therefore, the development of a rapid and sensitive detection method for the detection of ClO- in environment and food is desirable.

Up to now, many methods have been reported for ClO- determination, including iodine titration, electrochemistry, ion chromatography, chemiluminescence, colorimetry and fluorescence analysis. For example, Kumaravel et.al developed a ratiometric electrochemical molecular switch for sensing ClO- in food analysis of milk, orange juice and tea [26]. Costa et. al used colorimetric method to quantitatively analyze milk adulterants (starch, H2O2 and NaClO) [27]. Recently, fluorescence method has attracted much attention because of its high sensitivity, high selectivity, fast response, simplicity and easy-operation. For instance, Tan et.al designed a self-assemble nanoprobe with metal organic framework as a scaffold for ratiometric detection of ClO- [28]. Wang et.al developed a viable fluorescent water-soluble MoS2 quantum dots probe for the determination of ClO- [21]. Guo et. al designed a blue-emitting silicon quantum dots for fluorescent detection of ClO- in tap water [29]. However, the sensitivity and detection range still need to be improved. Therefore, more efforts should be devoted to develop sensitive fluorescence sensing system for ClO-.

CCM is a diketone compound extracted from the rhizome of some plants in the family of Zingiaceae and Araceae, with the functions of lowering blood lipids, anti-tumor, anti-inflammatory, choleric and anti-oxidation. Because of the interaction between CCM and ClO-, the fluorescence of CCM was quenched by ClO-. A fluorescence method for the detection of ClO- had been fabricated based on CCM. However, monochromatic fluorescence of CCM owes background fluorescence interference, narrow detection range and low detection limit. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a ratio-fluorescence probe to reduce the background, widen the detection channel and expand the application prospect.

In this work, CuNCs were explored to design the probe for ratio-fluorescence detection of CCM and ClO- based on inner filter effect (IFE). With the addition of CCM, the fluorescence of CuNCs was quenched by CCM via IFE and the fluorescence of CCM was enhanced gradually. The CuNCs were designed as ratiometric probe for detecting CCM. After adding ClO- in the CuNCs-CCM system, the fluorescence of Cu NCs recovered while the fluorescence of CCM quenched because the phenolic and methoxy groups of CCM were oxidized to quinones. Inspired by inhibition of IFE between CuNCs and CCM after oxidization by ClO-, an effective ratio-fluorescence sensor for the ClO- detection has been further fabricated. In addition, obvious color change can be observed in the presence of CCM and ClO-, which is helpful to further establish a naked eye sensor. The fluorescence color of the solution was collected through smartphone, and the RGB value was read by the color recognition software. Finally, by plotting the relationship curve between R/B and the concentration of the CCM and ClO- respectively, semi-quantitative analysis can be successfully realized. Thus, a naked-eye ratiometric and digital image colorimetric way to detect ClO- was developed based on the fluorescent and RGB signal. Moreover, smartphone-assisted ratio probe was successfully applied to the monitoring of ClO- additives in milk and water. This quick, convenient and sensitive method with broad linear range has high potential for ClO- in complex food matrix.

All reagents were of at least analytical grade. Cu(Cl)2·2H2O, lithium bromide (LiBr) was purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ovalbumin (OVA) was purchased from Shanghai yuanye Bio-Technology Co. Ltd. CCM, ascorbic Acid (AA), Glutathione (GSH), Glucose (Glu), Aluminium chloride (AlCl3), magnesium sulfate (MgSO4), calcium chloride (CaCl2), potassium chloride (KCl) were ordered from Aladdin Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sodium thiosulfate (Na2S2O3·5H2O) was purchased from Rand Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). Anhydrous ethanol, sodium hypochlorite (NaClO), L-cysteine (Cys), lysine (Lys), tyrosine, sodium chloride (NaCl) ,sodium nitrite (NaNO2), potassium bromide (KBr), potassium iodide (KI), sodium fluoride (NaF), zinc chloride (ZnCl2), ferrous sulfate (FeSO4·7H2O), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), hydrazine hydrate (N2H4·H2O) were purchased from Sinopharm Reagent Chemical Group Co. Ltd (Shanghai,China). barium chloride (BaCl2) was purchased from Tianjin Suzhuang Chemical Reagent Factory. Sodium hydroxide was purchased from Tianjin bodi chemical Co. Ltd. Ultrapure water was used throughout all study. All solutions were freshly prepared in deionized water under ambient conditions.

The fluorescence measurements were carried out on LS55 fluorescence spectrometer (Perkin-Elmer, USA). The UV–vis absorption measurements were performed on Lambda 35 UV analyzer (Perkin-Elmer, USA). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was conducted using a JEM-2100 UHR (JEOL, Japan). A Thermo Fisher Escalab 250Xi spectrometer was used for the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses. Fourier Transform Infra-Red (FTIR) spectra were taken with a Spectrum One FTIR spectrophotometer (Perkin-Elmer, USA) at room temperature. The centrifuge was TG 16-Ⅱ (Pingfan Instrument, China).

In a typical experiment, ovalbumin-protected Cu NCs were prepared by adding 1 mL CuCl2 solution (10 mM), ovalbumin solution (10 mg/mL, 4 mL) into a 10 mL vial and stirred for five minutes, followed by the addition of 150 μL N2H4·H2O solution (85%) and 300 μL NaOH solution (1 M). After reacting 16 h at room temperature, the mixed solution changed from yellow to purple, indicated the formation of Cu NCs. Then the product was purified by dialysis for 8 h (the cutoff of the dialysis membrane was equivalent to Mw = 500). The as-prepared Cu NCs were stored at 4◦C for long-term preservation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratio- fluorescence detection of CCM with Cu NCs
ce:section.ce:para: The analytical process for CCM detection was performed as follows. This fluorescent assay was carried out in 2 mL of ethanol solution. The solution of CCM with different volumes were fixed into 60 μL Cu NCs solution with the CCM concentration of 0–5 μM. Fluorescence spectra were measured at excitation wavelength of 340 nm, voltage of 800 V and slit width of 15/20 nm. The emission intensity ratio of Cu NCs-CCM at 436 and 536 nm (F536/F436) was recorded for the quantitative analysis of CCM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence assay of ClO- with Cu NCs-CCM system
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of ClO- was performed in 2 mL anhydrous ethanol. 60 μL Cu NCs solution and 5 μM CCM solution were taken in glass bottle. Different concentrations of ClO- (from 0 to 412 μM) were added to the mixture for 3 min, the emission spectra of these mixture solutions were recorded in the range of 360 to 650 nm upon excitating at 340 nm. The emission intensity ratio of Cu NCs-CCM at 436 and 536 nm (F536/F436) was used for the quantitative analysis of ClO-.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity analysis for CCM and ClO-
ce:section.ce:para: Same experimental procedures were adopted for the selectivity study, but ClO- was replaced by other reactive oxygen species or interferential substances with same concentration, including NO2–, S2O3 2-, IO3 2-, H2O2, HO·, F-, Cl-, Br-, I-. The selectivity of CCM through detecting certain substances including 50 µM of K+, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Mn2+, Ba2+, GSH, AA, Glu, Lys, Cys, and Try under the same conditions. The fluorescence spectra were measured with an excitation wavelength at 340 nm and emission wavelength at 436 and 536 nm. All error bars represent standard deviations from three repeated experiments.

As shown in Fig. 1 a, emission peaks at 438 nm were recorded at the optimum excitation wavelength of 340 nm. Strong blue luminescence was also observed under UV light at 365 nm (In the inset of Fig. 1a). Meanwhile, the quantum yield of Cu NCs was calculated as 4.9% using rhodamine B as standard reference. In order to obtain Cu NCs with strong fluorescence, synthesis conditions such as concentration of Cu2+ and OVA, the amount of reducing agent and sodium hydroxide, and reaction time were taken into consideration (Fig. S1). As can be seen from Fig. S2, the fluorescence intensity of Cu NCs barely changed after one month's storage. And the fluorescence intensity of Cu NCs did not change significantly under alkaline conditions (Fig. S3).

The formation of Cu NCs was characterized using fluorescence, TEM, FTIR and XPS. As shown in Fig. 1a, the maximum fluorescence excitation and emission wavelength of ovalbumin protected Cu NCs were 340 nm and 438 nm. The TEM image in Fig. 1b showed that the Cu NCs was excellent monodispersed. The diameter of the Cu NCs is about 2.5 nm. Besides, the HRTEM image shown in the inset of Fig. 1b displayed the highly crystalline structure of the Cu NCs with the lattice fringe spacing of 0.276 nm. According to Fig. 1c, the FTIR spectra of the OVA-protected Cu NCs is similar to that of the OVA, and compared with the FTIR spectra of OVA, the peak position of the characteristic absorption of C-N (2255 cm−1) was disappeared in the spectra of Cu NCs, indicating the OVA combined on the surface of Cu NCs through Cu-N bond. Furthermore, XPS measurement was performed to confirm the chemical state and composition of the Cu NCs. Two distinct peaks observed at 932.6 eV and 952.3 eV assigned to be the binding energies of 2p3/2 and 2p2/1 electrons of Cu (0) and Cu (I), respectively (Fig. 1d). This result indicates that Cu NCs mainly consist of Cu (0) and Cu (I), with Cu (II) absent in the nanoclusters [30].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle for CCM and ClO- analysis using Cu NCs
ce:section.ce:para: The optimal excitation wavelength and maximum fluorescence emission peak of CCM were at 430 nm and 536 nm, respectively (Fig. S4). The color of CCM is yellow under natural light, yellow-green fluorescence appears under UV light as shown in the inset of Fig. S4. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the absorption peak of CCM locates at 430 nm, which highly overlapped with the fluorescence emission spectrum of the CuNCs. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , in the presence of CCM, the fluorescence of Cu NCs at 435 nm can be quenched because of the inner filter effect (IFE) while the fluorescence of CCM at 536 nm was enhanced. After addition of ClO- sequencely, ClO- can oxidize the o-methoxyphenol of CCM into benzoquinone, which leads to the decrease of absorbance of CCM at 430 nm. Notably, the overlap between the absorption spectra of CCM and the fluorescence emission of CuNCs decreases, which inhibits the effect of IFE between CCM and CuNCs. Thus, the fluorescence quenching of CCM at 536 nm was quenched and the fluorescence of CuNCs can be restored.

As shown in Fig. 2b, when CCM was added to Cu NCs solution, the fluorescence of Cu NCs was quenched and fluorescence of CCM was enhanced. Upon the addition of ClO- into Cu NCs-CCM solution, the fluorescence of CCM was quenched significantly, while the fluorescence of Cu NCs displays an obvious increase. However, the addition of ClO- has a particularly small influence on the fluorescence of Cu NCs even its concentration reached to 400 µM. In the control experiment, the fluorescence spectrum of CCM has been displayed as Fig. 3 . The fluorescence emission of CCM is located at 536 nm. After the addition of ClO-, the fluorescence of CCM was quenched obviously due to the oxidation between ClO- and curcumin. In our detection system, Cu NCs were used as the reference of the ratio sensing. Therefore, this dual response mode for ClO- can effectively reduce the background interference and improve the detection sensitivity and reliability.

Fluorescence response of Cu NCs for detecting CCM were investigated in Fig. 4 a. With the increase of the concentration of CCM varying from 0 to 5 μM, the fluorescence of Cu NCs at 438 nm gradually decreases and the fluorescence of CCM at 536 nm gradually increases. The value of F536/F436) have a linear relationship with the concentration of CCM from 0 to 5 μM as shown in Fig. 4b with the linear equation is F536/F436 = 0.7554C + 0.1072, C refers to the concentration of CCM. The detection limit is 17 nM according to LOD = 3ϭ/s, where ϭ is the standard deviation of the blank and s is the slope of the calibration plot. Fig. 4c showed a series of photographs of the sensing system with different concentrations of CCM taken under daylight and 365 nm UV light. With the increase of CCM concentration, the color of the mixed system solution gradually changes from colorless to yellow. And the fluorescence color gradually changes from the original blue to yellow. The above results proved the feasibility of naked-eye and semi-quantitative detection detection of CCM.

To test the selectivity of the developed sensing system, several potential substances were determined under the same conditions including NO2–, S2O3 2-, IO3 2-, H2O2, HO·, F-, Cl-, Br-, I-. It was observed from Fig. 6 that the ratio fluorescence intensity of the sensing system changed obviously with the addition of ClO-. While all the other substances used above did not changed such obvious ratio fluorescence change in comparison. This phenomenon manifested that CuNCs-CCM based sensing system has good selectivity for ClO-, indicating its wide applications in complex samples. In addition, we also compared the selectivity of the sensor system based on CuNCs to detect CCM, as shown in Fig. S6, also indicating that CuNCs has a good selectivity for CCM response.

Under UV light, the developed probe clearly shows the solution color changes from blue to yellow and from yellow to blue at the presence of different concentrations of CCM and ClO- respectively. As shown in the Fig. 3c and 4e, images were collected via Huawei smartphones. Color recognizer software was used to identify the color of RGB values and origin software was used for data processing. By plotting the relationship curve between R/B and the concentration of the CCM and ClO- respectively, two good linear relationship were obtained in the range of 0.5–5 µM and 5–150 µM respectively. As is shown in Fig. 7 a and Fig. 7b, the linear regression equations are R/B = 0.24343CCCM + 0.26284 (R2 = 0.986) and R/B = -0.00357CClO -+1.36416 (R2 = 0.973), the detection limits are 0.06 µM and 0.5 µM respectively, indicating the reliability and practicality of smartphone semiquantitative detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ClO- in tap water and milk
ce:section.ce:para: Tap water was collected in our laboratory and was filtered through a 0.22 µm filter. The milk, Mengniu pure milk from a local walmart, was pretreated with acetic acid and filtered with a 0.22 µm filter. Then standard ClO- was added to prepare spiked sample respectively. Similar to the procedure for detecting ClO- solution. The results by fluorescence and smartphone application were summarized in Table S1 and able S2. The fluorescence method recoveries of ClO- in the water samples and milk are in the range of 93.2 to 99.6%. The smartphone application method recoveries of ClO- in the water samples and milk are in the range of 97.2 to 113.5%. This indicates that smartphone-assisted visual ratio-fluorescence detection method is reliable and feasibility.

In summary, Cu NCs with good stability was successfully synthesized. Based on this, a quickly and sensitive sensing method has been established for ClO- determination by taking advantage of the oxidation reaction between CCM and ClO- in the CuNCs-CCM dual mode system. And the color change during the detecting procedure is easily observed by naked eye, which is beneficial for semi-quantitative analysis. Furthermore, the Cu NCs-CCM sensing system showed great selectivity toward ClO- compared with other substances. Excitingly, the smartphone-assisted detection method obtained considerable results, which provides a convenient way for the intelligent application of analysis. Moreover, the smartphone-assisted ratiometric probe was successfully applied to the monitoring of hypochlorite additives in milk and the detection of hypochlorite in water. This work provides a successful example for their potential application in food and environment samples based on copper nanoclusters, which is expected to widen the application areas of metal nanoclusters. The introduction of smart devices will also exert greater application potential in the field of analysis.

Yanyue Li: Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Yu He: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition. Yili Ge: Visualization, Investigation. Gongwu Song: Data curation, Project administration. Jiangang Zhou: Validation, Resources, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declared that there is no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21707030).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.119740.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
